--- SOURCE 3: Influence of fertilizer and manure inputs on soil health: A review ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297

SUMMARY:
In another study, the addition of NPK fertilizers increased the amount of SOC derived from fresh biomass inputs and decreased the priming effect intensity in a Chinese maize cropping system (Wu. et al., 2019). Despite the recent acceptance of POxC as a soil health indicator and its increased use in research studies since 2003 (Culman et al., 2012; Hurisso et al., 2016; Calderon et al., 2017), there is little known about what POxC most closely represents (Culman et al., 2012); it is generally considered to be strongly correlated with SOC, particulate organic C (POC), and microbial biomass C (Culman et al., 2012). Zhang, _et al._ Increased soil aggregate stability by altering contents and chemical composition of organic carbon fractions via seven years of manure addition in mollisols  Agric, 13 (1) (2023), p.

Influence of fertilizer and manure inputs on soil health: A review
==================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Julie A.Howe a, Mark D.McDonald a b, Joseph Burke a b, Isaiah Robertson a, Harrison Coker a, Terry J.Gentry a, Katie L.Lewis b

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.soisec.2024.100155](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.soisec.2024.100155 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2667006224000297&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/)

Open access

Abstract
--------

Sustainable crop production requires nutrient inputs to replace what was removed or lost. Replenishing nutrients using fertilizers and animal wastes is one of the most common input practices. While there is considerable research evaluating the efficacy of fertilizer and manure-based inputs for crop performance, [nutrient use efficiency](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/nutrient-use-efficiency "Learn more about nutrient use efficiency from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and the subsequent impact to the environment, the influence of these inputs on soil health properties is less understood. The goals of this review are to summarize the state of literature on the influence of [chemical fertilizer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/chemical-fertilizer "Learn more about chemical fertilizer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and manure-based inputs on soil health and to identify research gaps to refine efforts toward improved soil health, sustainability, and [soil carbon](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-carbon "Learn more about soil carbon from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") storage. Conclusions suggest that chemical fertilizer inputs can increase [soil organic matter](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-organic-matter "Learn more about soil organic matter from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and soil health provided that additions are not beyond what a plant can utilize. Manure-based inputs contribute to [soil organic carbon](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-organic-carbon "Learn more about soil organic carbon from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and associated metrics; however, direct comparison of products is difficult as they are generally applied on a nutrient basis rather than carbon basis, which differentially affects their influence on soil health. Overall, nutrient-containing inputs show beneficial influences on soil health metrics; however, more focused research into their influence on soil health is needed.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000285)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000340)

Keywords
--------

Soil health

Fertilizer

Manure

Soil organic carbon

Compost

1. Introduction
---------------

Crop production necessitates the removal of plant biomass through harvest or animal consumption, which includes nutrients contained in their tissues. When repeated over time, the soil is depleted of nutrients because biochemical reactions cannot adequately resupply nutrients lost at harvest rapidly enough to satisfy the subsequent crop's demand. Thus, nutrient management is a critical component of any sustainable crop production system. Nutrient input through the application of fertilizers and animal wastes is the most common technique employed to return what was removed through harvest. While there is considerable research evaluating the efficacy of nutrient inputs for yield and overall impacts to the environment ([Xia et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0133); [Schmitt et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0105); [Blaise et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0009); [Stewart et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0114); [Hawkesford, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0051); [Ronner et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0102)), the influence of these inputs on soil health properties is poorly understood. The objectives of this review are to summarize the state of literature on the influence of nutrient inputs, such as fertilizer and manure, on soil health and to identify research gaps that may allow refinement of our knowledge of soil health enhancing practices.

2. Influence of fertilizer on soil health
-----------------------------------------

Fertilizers derived from mineral sources or industrial processes (referred to chemical fertilizers) are relatively pure sources of plant nutrients. The majority of these products are concentrated sources of soluble and plant-available nutrients that have a relatively low or no carbon (C) content ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#tbl0001)). While some [mineral fertilizers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/mineral-fertilizers "Learn more about mineral fertilizers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are derived from mining (e.g., rock phosphate, sulfur), some fertilizers are produced through industrial processes. The energy-intensive Haber-Bosch process is the source for most nitrogen (N) fertilizers used today ([Cherkasov et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0023)). This process converts N gas (N 2) and hydrogen gas (H 2) into ammonia (NH 3), which is subsequently converted into ammonium (NH 4+) and nitrate (NO 3−) fertilizer materials. Common fertilizers derived from the Haber-Bosch process include [ammonium nitrate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/ammonium-nitrate "Learn more about ammonium nitrate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), calcium nitrate, potassium nitrate, urea, monoammonium phosphate, and [diammonium phosphate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/diammonium-phosphate "Learn more about diammonium phosphate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#tbl0001)). Phosphorus (P) fertilizers are typically produced from acidification of phosphate rock. In addition to the ammonium-based phosphate fertilizers, triple super phosphate is also used. Potassium (K) fertilizers are generated from the refinement of K salts or ores and in some cases by solution mining. The most common is muriate of potash, which is potassium chloride.

Table 1. Common chemical fertilizer materials and their formulas.

| Fertilizer | Chemical Composition | Fertilizer | Chemical Composition |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Anhydrous ammonia | NH 3 | Triple super phosphate | Ca(H 2 PO 4)2 |
| Ammonium nitrate | NH 4 NO 3 | Muriate of potash | KCl |
| Calcium nitrate | Ca(NO 3)2 | Urea | (NH 2)2 CO |
| Diammonium phosphate | (NH 4)2 H 2 PO 4 | Potassium nitrate | KNO 3 |
| Monoammonium phosphate | NH 4 H 2 PO 4 |  |  |

Conventional plant production systems (i.e., non-organic production systems) rely on fertilizers to increase yield and return nutrients removed during harvest. The efficiency of fertilizers varies considerably by nutrient, crop, and local conditions at application. For N, efficiency is generally low. Nitrogen use efficiency (NUE) is defined as the amount of N applied to the amount of N removed at harvest. Using isotopes, [Dourado-Neto et al. (2010)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0033) evaluated fertilizer N uptake in 13 diverse tropical agroecosystems and reported that 7–58 % of the applied fertilizer N was absorbed by the plant. Poor NUE contributes to fertilizer losses to the environment, particularly in water ways, which leads to eutrophication or harmful blooms of [aquatic organisms](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/aquatic-organism "Learn more about aquatic organisms from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that lead to “dead zones” or low oxygen environments. Excessive [fertilizer application](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/fertilizer-application "Learn more about fertilizer application from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") rates, especially with poor NUE fertilizers, that surpass plant uptake may exacerbate this problem.

The effect of fertilizers on cropping systems has primarily focused on crop yield and their environmental fate ([Xia et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0133); [Schmitt et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0105); [Blaise et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0009); [Stewart et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0114); [Hawkesford, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0051); [Ronner et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0102)). The impact of fertilizers on soil health is not as well established, but a few reviews and meta-analyses have sought to address this gap in the literature ([Grandy et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0045); [Krasilnikov et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0067); Singh et al., 2018; [Geisseler and Scow, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0044); [Geisseler et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0042)). To complicate this further, few long-term studies have assessed chemical, physical, and biological soil health properties with various levels of fertilization ([Khan et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0063); [Poffenbarger et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0095); [Dhiman et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0030); [Duo et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0034)).

### 2.1. Fertilizer: influence on soil organic carbon

Soil organic carbon (SOC) is considered one of the best indicators of soil health because of its ability to influence multiple soil properties and affect soil function. The 24 h potential C mineralization assessment and soil organic C content are recommended as the two best measures of soil health ([Liptzin et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0077)). Numerous studies and meta-analyses of research conducted on a wide range of crops around the world generally show that N fertilizer inputs increase SOC compared to no N inputs in cultivated systems ([Tang et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0116); [Dhiman et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0030); [Geisseler and Scow, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0044); [Geisseler et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0042); [Singh, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0110); [Duo et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0034)). The resulting increase in crop growth from fertilizer inputs contributes to greater surface residue and root biomass that increase SOC over time.

A meta-analysis of 107 long-term fertilization studies around the world reported that fertilization increased SOC compared to an unfertilized control by an average of 12.8 % ([Geisseler and Scow, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0044)). Of those 107 studies evaluated, only 18 reported a decrease in SOC ([Geisseler and Scow, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0044)). [Poffenbarger et al. (2017)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0095) and [Dhiman et al. (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0030) conducted studies with applied N at 0, less than optimum, optimum, and excessive rates in long-term studies (i.e., 14–16 years and 44 years, respectively). Both studies found that SOC was greatest when N [fertilizer rate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/fertilizer-rates "Learn more about fertilizer rate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was agronomically optimum in non-legume cropping systems. [Dhiman et al. (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0030) used established N, P, and K recommended rates for their wheat cropping system, but [Poffenbarger et al. (2017)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0095) determined agronomically optimum N rates for each cropping system (i.e., continuous maize and maize-soybean rotation) in four distinct regions in Iowa, which were based on 14-to-16-year averages for 7 N rates. With the N rates scaled relative to optimum N for each region and cropping system, [Poffenbarger et al. (2017)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0095) found that SOC increased up to the optimum rate, then slightly decreased in the continuous maize system, while the maize-soybean system had a slight, but not significant, increase in SOC with N rate. A study by [Lu et al. (2011)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0082) found that SOC decreased with N addition in forests and grasslands, but it was significantly increased by 3.6 % in agricultural ecosystems.

When N is applied in excess of the agronomically optimum yield, plant growth can shift to [vegetative growth](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/vegetative-growth "Learn more about vegetative growth from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") at the expense of yield ([Albornoz, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0002); [Marschner, 1995](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0085)). Despite the potential gains in vegetative growth, SOC from excessive N fertilization was less than the optimum N rate and greater than the no N control ([Singh, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0110); [Poffenbarger et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0095); [Khan et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0063)). Excess N is thought to reduce the limitation of N associated with organic matter degradation either due to additional exogenous N or to lowering the C:N ratio of residues ([Singh, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0110); [Poffenbarger et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0095)). However, repartitioning of [photosynthates](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/photosynthate "Learn more about photosynthates from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to [aboveground biomass](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/aboveground-biomass "Learn more about aboveground biomass from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") over [belowground biomass](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/belowground-biomass "Learn more about belowground biomass from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is known to occur as well ([Marschner, 1995](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0085)), which may also indicate the importance of root biomass in SOC, which has been suggested in other studies ([Rasse et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0099); [Burke et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0017); [Lu et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0082)).

An increase in the rate of SOM degradation (i.e., priming effect) has been suggested to result from N fertilization in some systems ([Kuzyakov et al., 2000](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0069)), although the phenomenon has not been thoroughly explored in published long-term field experiments. Nitrogen fertilization decreased SOM priming and emissions of carbon dioxide (CO 2) and methane (CH 4) compared to unfertilized plots in a subtropical rice production system ([Zhu et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0141)). [Zhu et al. (2018)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0141) attributed the decrease in SOM priming to a decrease in [microbial biomass](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/microbial-biomass "Learn more about microbial biomass from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with increased N fertilizer. In another study, the addition of [NPK fertilizers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/npk-fertilizers "Learn more about NPK fertilizers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") increased the amount of SOC derived from fresh biomass inputs and decreased the priming effect intensity in a Chinese maize cropping system ([Wu. et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0132)). In woodlands, however, the priming effect was stimulated by a range of N application rates that reduced SOC throughout the soil profile ([Zheng et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0139)). A review by [Bünemann et al. (2006)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0016) found little evidence for a priming effect and suggested that maybe it is short-lived.

Permanganate oxidizable carbon (POxC), also commonly referred to as active C or reactive C, is a relatively new method for assessing a fraction of SOC that is believed to be readily available to microorganisms during the mineralization of SOM ([Weil et al., 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0129)). Although it was proposed as a measure of soil quality in 1995, POxC was not incorporated into the initial NRCS Soil Quality Assessment ([Blair et al., 1995](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0008); [USDA-NRCS, 2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0121)). More recently, the Cornell Comprehensive Assessment for Soil Health (CASH) and the new NRCS Soil Health Assessment highlight POxC as an important soil health indicator ([Moebius-Clune et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0090); [USDA-NRCS, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0123)). Despite the recent acceptance of POxC as a soil health indicator and its increased use in research studies since 2003 ([Culman et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0026); [Hurisso et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0057); [Calderon et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0020)), there is little known about what POxC most closely represents ([Culman et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0026)); it is generally considered to be strongly correlated with SOC, particulate organic C (POC), and microbial biomass C ([Culman et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0026)).

Comparison and interpretation of POxC values amongst soils is somewhat unclear. Normalizing POxC to SOC has been suggested to improve comparability of POxC across soil types particularly when SOC is highly variable; however, this limits the potential to use POxC as a standalone indicator ([Pulleman et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0096)). Another study, which includes some of the same authors, suggests that there are few benefits to normalizing POxC to SOC and instead suggests that POxC be reported strictly in terms of MnO 4− reduced (i.e., µmol MnO 4− reduced kg−1) ([Wade et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0125)). Overall, there is no clear consensus of the pool of C that is evaluated by POxC, nor is there an agreed mechanism to report and interpret POxC values among soils; however, its strong correlation with SOC, POC, and microbial biomass C suggests it has strong applicability as an assessment of soil health.

Few studies have evaluated the influence of fertilization on soil POxC concentrations. In those that have been conducted, POxC levels generally increased with added N fertilizer application and results were consistent with increases in SOC ([Mirsky et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0088); [Wang et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0126); [Zhang et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0136).

The relationship between POxC and other soil health indicators is still not fully understood. [Weil et al. (2003)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0129) and [Culman et al. (2012)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0026) reported that POxC was highly correlated to SOC, while [Culman et al. (2012)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0026) reported that it was more similar to smaller-sized POC and microbial biomass C than SOC. Some have reported that POxC might overestimate the available SOC fraction making it more similar to total organic C ([Aziz et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0005)). Others have found little relationship between POxC and mineralizable C, which further confounds the exact meaning of POxC ([Hurisso et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0057); [Burke et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0017)). SOC and POxC fluctuate significantly during the [growing season](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/growing-season "Learn more about growing season from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Wuest, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib142); [Burke et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0017); [Lazicki et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667006224000297#bib0071)), which has been attributed to [rhizodeposition](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/rhizodeposition "Learn more about rhizodeposition from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") from the actively growing crop. These studies highlight how factors, such as plant growth stage, influence changes in POxC and SOC and furthermore the importance of choosing a [soil sampling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-sampling "Learn more about soil sampling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") time.

### 2.2. Fertilizer: soil chemical properties

Soil pH is considered a master variable due to its influence on a wide range of soil properties. Plants of differing growth strategies perform best in defined soil pH ranges, with most agricultural crops preferring slightly acid soils to mildly alkaline (i.e., pH 6 – 7.5). Chemically, soil pH is a major driver of nutrient and non-nutrient element solubility and speciation in soil. For example, many plant nutrients (e.g., Fe, Cu, and Mn) are more soluble, and thus more plant available, when pH is low. Other soil elements (e.g., Al) may become toxic to plants at low pH. The bioavailability of these elements to plants arises typically by shifts from soluble cationic forms (e.g., Fe 3+, Cu 2+, Mn 2+, Al 3+, Al(OH)2+, Al(OH)2+) at low pH to insoluble minerals (e.g., Fe(OH)3(s), MnO 2(s), Cu(OH)2(s), and Al(OH)3(s)) as pH increases. In addition, pH affects the charge properties of both mineral and organic [soil colloids](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-colloid "Learn more about soil colloids from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Most colloids have a net negative charge and adsorb cations for charge balance. Because the cations are electrostatically bound, the cations are exchangeable, and the capacity of soil to hold cations is called the cation exchange capacity (CEC). The most common cations affected by changes in CEC ar

enriched_points.expanded_content: Chemical fertilizers can degrade soil structure, lead to nutrient imbalances, and increase soil acidity, resulting in long-term productivity loss and reduced soil health.
Chemical applications harm soil organisms, including bacteria, fungi, and earthworms, disrupting the ecosystem and diminishing the soil's biological diversity critical for nutrient cycling.
Over-reliance on chemical inputs can lead to diminishing returns in crop productivity, as soil degradation affects nutrient availability and crop resilience to pests and diseases.
Excessive use of chemical fertilizers contributes to waterway pollution through runoff, causing eutrophication and harmful algal blooms, which threaten aquatic ecosystems and human health.
